original_hypothesis.title: Relayer Gas Griefing Fee Theft via Insufficient Gas in processMessage
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: A malicious relayer can call processMessage with deliberately insufficient gas. The EVM's 63/64 rule causes excessivelySafeCall to forward less gas than _message.gasLimit to the target, causing the invocation to fail. The message is marked RETRIABLE and the relayer pockets the entire fee, while the user must pay gas again to retry with no fee incentive for retriers.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The code does not validate that gasleft() is sufficient to forward _message.gasLimit before calling _invokeMessageCall. On L2 (invocationDelay=0), proof and execution happen atomically in one transaction, giving the relayer full control. This is exploitable by any address without special privileges.

proof_of_signal.pattern: gasLimit = msg.sender == _message.destOwner ? gasleft() : _message.gasLimit; No check that gasleft() >= _message.gasLimit before forwarding to excessivelySafeCall

preconditions: Message with non-zero fee exists and is proven on destination chain
Relayer is not the destOwner (uses _message.gasLimit path)
On L2 chain (invocationDelay=0) for single-tx atomic exploitation
Message gasLimit is significantly higher than tx overhead gas cost

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Relayer provides tx gas = overhead + small buffer; EVM 63/64 rule gives call min(gasLimit, 63/64 * gasleft) which is far below _message.gasLimit
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Relayer profit = message.fee - tx_gas_cost. For messages with reasonable fees (>0.01 ETH) and low gas overhead (~200k gas), profit margin is significant

economic_questions: What is the typical fee-to-gas-cost ratio for bridge messages?
How many RETRIABLE messages exist that were never retried?
Is there a minimum fee enforced at the application layer?
validation_requirements: Construct PoC: processMessage with gas = overhead + 50k, verify message becomes RETRIABLE
Measure exact gas overhead for processMessage excluding the external call
Calculate break-even fee amount for griefing attack

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: ~0.01 ETH gas cost for tx submission
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: message.fee minus gas cost per message
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on fee; potentially 50-90% of fee value

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time messages with fees are pending on L2
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact, pure fee extraction

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users who send bridge messages with non-zero fees
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full loss of message fee + cost to retry
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Aggregate of all pending message fees

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: gasLimit values in typical bridge messages, fee amounts
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify 63/64 rule causes silent failure for external call

validation_result.mechanism: Relayer gas griefing via insufficient gas forwarding
validation_result.impact_summary: A malicious relayer can call processMessage with insufficient gas, causing the external message call to fail silently due to EVM's 63/64 rule. The message becomes RETRIABLE, but the relayer pockets the full fee. Users lose their fee and must pay again to retry.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The processMessage function in Bridge.sol sets gasLimit = _message.gasLimit when the caller is not the destOwner (the standard relayer path). This value is passed to _invokeMessageCall, which forwards it to ExcessivelySafeCall.excessivelySafeCall. However, the EVM's call opcode applies the 63/64 rule: it forwards at most min(_gas, 63/64 * gasleft()) gas. If the relayer provides a transaction with gas sufficient only for processMessage overhead (proof verification, storage operations) but far below _message.gasLimit, the actual gas forwarded to the target call will be much less than _message.gasLimit. The target call fails due to OOG. ExcessivelySafeCall returns success=false without reverting, and processMessage marks the message as RETRIABLE and pays the full fee to the relayer (msg.sender). On L2 where invocationDelay=0, proof and execution happen atomically in one transaction, giving the relayer complete control over gas supply. There is no check anywhere in processMessage or _invokeMessageCall that gasleft() >= _message.gasLimit before making the external call.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible in two locations: (1) Bridge.sol line ~217 where gasLimit is set to _message.gasLimit without validating available gas, and (2) _invokeMessageCall which passes this value directly to excessivelySafeCall without any gas availability check. The fee payment to msg.sender (the relayer) occurs unconditionally after the invocation attempt regardless of success/failure of the external call.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When msg.sender != _message.destOwner (relayer path), gasLimit is set to _message.gasLimit without checking that gasleft() is sufficient to actually forward that much gas. Due to EVM's 63/64 rule, the actual gas forwarded is min(_message.gasLimit, 63/64 * gasleft()), which can be far less than _message.gasLimit if the relayer supplies minimal transaction gas.

The function checks _gasLimit != 0 but does not verify that gasleft() is sufficient to actually forward _gasLimit gas. The excessivelySafeCall passes _gasLimit to the EVM call opcode, but the EVM caps forwarded gas to 63/64 of gasleft().

The fee is paid to the relayer (msg.sender) unconditionally after the message invocation attempt, regardless of whether the external call succeeded or failed. A relayer that deliberately causes failure still receives the full fee.

calling_sequence: 1. Relayer calls processMessage with gas = overhead + small buffer (e.g., ~300k total)
2. hashMessage computes msgHash, status verified as NEW
4. On L2: invocationDelay=0, so execution proceeds immediately
9. EVM call opcode applies 63/64 rule: forwards min(1000000, 63/64 * gasleft())
10. At this point gasleft() might be ~100k, so only ~98k gas is forwarded
11. Target call fails due to OOG with only ~98k instead of needed 1,000,000
17. Remaining gas is sufficient for status update and ETH transfer (~50k gas)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical missing check is between step 5 and step 6. After setting gasLimit = _message.gasLimit, there should be a check like `require(gasleft() >= _message.gasLimit + OVERHEAD)` to ensure the EVM can actually forward the requested amount. Without this check, the relayer controls how much gas the target call actually receives by controlling the transaction gas limit.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The getInvocationDelays function shows L2 chains (non-L1, non-testnet) return (0, 0), confirming atomic proof+execution. Deployment scripts deploy Bridge to both L1 and L2.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is straightforward: (1) Monitor for bridge messages with non-trivial fees on L2. (2) When found, call processMessage with a transaction gas limit of approximately: proof_verification_cost + processMessage_overhead + small_buffer (e.g., 300k total). (3) The Merkle proof verification succeeds (consumes ~100-200k gas). (4) At _invokeMessageCall, gasleft() is only ~50-100k. (5) The 63/64 rule caps forwarded gas well below _message.gasLimit. (6) Target call fails silently. (7) Message becomes RETRIABLE. (8) Relayer receives _message.fee. Cost to attacker: ~300k gas * gas_price. Profit: _message.fee minus gas cost. For any message with fee > gas cost of ~300k gas, the attack is profitable.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH impact. Every bridge message with a non-zero fee on L2 is vulnerable. Users lose their fee and must manually retry (retryMessage has no fee incentive for third parties). The attack is permissionless, requires no special privileges, and is profitable for any message where fee > attacker's gas cost. This undermines the entire relayer incentive mechanism of the bridge.
recommended_mitigations: Add a gas check before _invokeMessageCall: require(gasleft() >= _message.gasLimit + PROCESSING_OVERHEAD, 'insufficient gas')

Alternative: Check gasleft() inside _invokeMessageCall before the excessivelySafeCall and revert if insufficient, preventing the message from being marked RETRIABLE when the relayer didn't provide enough gas
Consider only paying the fee proportional to actual gas forwarded, or not paying the fee if the call failed due to insufficient gas forwarding
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Bridge and SignalService on a test L2 chain (chainId not 1/testnets). 2. Send a bridge message with gasLimit=1,000,000 and fee=1 ETH. 3. Sync the signal on the destination chain. 4. Call processMessage from a different address (not destOwner) with tx gas = 300,000. 5. Verify: message status is RETRIABLE, relayer received 1 ETH fee, target contract was not properly executed.
uncertainties: Exact gas overhead for processMessage (depends on proof complexity, storage operations, chain ID) needs measurement to determine minimum profitable fee
Whether existing relayer infrastructure already validates gas sufficiency off-chain (doesn't fix the on-chain vulnerability but may reduce practical exploitation)
additional_testing_needed: Measure exact gas consumption of processMessage excluding the external call to determine minimum attack gas
Calculate break-even fee amount for different L2 gas prices
Test whether the remaining gas after a failed call is always sufficient to complete status update and fee payment
Verify behavior with different proof sizes (single-hop vs multi-hop) affecting overhead gas
